species.notes: W. Suarez (30-Jun-24): Alocasia boyceana and A. ramosii can overlap with A. heterophylla in the length of the posterior lobes relative to the anterior lobe, but the primary vein count of A. boyceana is always higher (5-7) than that of A. heterophylla, with obscure secondary veining vs. prominent and widely arching veins. The submarginals of both A. boyceana and A. ramosii are much closer to the leaf margins than those of A. heterophylla. Alocasia ramosii has typically pale green or black petioles, without markings, A. heterophylla has petioles with a dark grey base with rather inconspicuous dashes. Only A. boyceana has petioles with a pale base with dark dashes. Some A. boyceana plants can have blackish petioles similar to some A. heterophylla, but the leaf veining is different. 150-400m (photos).

species.notes: P.B. Pelser (29-Aug-20): Philippine plants of this taxon are also sometimes referred to as Homalomena pygmaea. Homalomena pygmaea and Homalomena humilis are two widely applied names for plants belonging to the Homalomena humils/pygmaea complex, for which currently no satisfactory species delimitation exists (Boyce & Wong 2009). Pending further study, we here follow Kurniawan et al. (2011) and list this complex as a single morphologically variable species of which the earliest name in Homalomena is Homalomena humilis.
